reasoning: Candidate A has the stronger direct conversion mechanic because the hero contains the actual free-alert setup form, which better matches the primary business action. But Candidate B wins overall because it resolves the core trust objection earlier and more cleanly: the hero sample alert makes the product tangible, the no-login and official-handoff language sit next to the CTA, and the mobile first viewport keeps the CTA visible while A's mobile path pushes the CTA below the first screen and has detected horizontal overflow. B is also more faithful to its assigned example-alert-proof strategy; A's workflow-first rationale is mostly deferred below the fold and the rendered first viewport reads closer to action-first. Programmatically, both fail, but A fails harder with multiple missing/unjustified components and mobile overflow, while B's failure is mainly a spec count/component artifact issue.
best_reusable_move_a: The embedded hero alert widget is the best conversion move: restaurant, dates, party size, channel, and Start Free Alert are all visible as a concrete task path instead of a generic CTA.
best_reusable_move_b: The hero sample alert card is the best trust move: it shows a believable notification with restaurant, party size, time, official booking link, and latency cue before asking the user to commit.
